title: Lumen — a from-scratch path tracer
description: A physically based path tracer built from scratch in TypeScript. NEWEST (15.0) — SPECTRAL SUBSURFACE SCATTERING (A CHROMATIC MEAN FREE PATH): the reason a hand held over a torch glows deep red at the edges is NOT that red is absorbed less per bounce — it is that red light TRAVELS FURTHER inside the flesh before it is absorbed at all. In skin, marble and milk the EXTINCTION ITSELF is chromatic (red low, blue high), so red reaches the thin edges while blue scatters back out near the surface. Lumen's 12.0 subsurface walk had a SCALAR mean free path — one σ_t for every colour — so all the chromatic character had to come from the per-collision albedo; it could not express this. 15.0 adds it by reusing the HERO-WAVELENGTH machinery that already disperses glass and colours real metals: a path that refracts into a SPECTRAL interior commits to one wavelength λ, takes its RGB weight ONCE (E_λ[w]=(1,1,1), so the estimator stays UNBIASED — colour is only a spread across λ, never a change of mean), then random-walks MONOCHROMATICALLY with the extinction σ_t(λ) and single-scattering albedo ϖ(λ) resolved at that wavelength, the chromatic translucency reconstructing over many paths' wavelengths. Nothing in the transport loop changes except WHICH scalar σ_t/ϖ the interior free-flight uses, so the scalar 12.0 walk is kept bit-for-bit for any non-spectral interior and all 75 prior proofs stay green. The media are not hand-tuned: a new MEASURED BSSRDF LIBRARY carries the canonical coefficients of Jensen, Marschner, Levoy & Hanrahan (SIGGRAPH 2001, Table 2) — marble, skin, whole & skim milk, cream, ketchup, chicken, potato, apple — converting their reduced σ_s′ and absorption σ_a into the per-wavelength extinction the walk consumes, so the new Living Skin (a measured-skin figurine raked from behind so its thin edges bleed red) and Apothecary (a shelf of spheres of real milk/marble/ketchup/cream, no two alike) scenes render REAL measured data. Six new proofs (81 total): the RGB→wavelength upsampling reproduces its control points and stays in envelope; every measured medium decodes to σ_t>0, ϖ∈[0,1] with red the deepest-penetrating for skin/marble; a chromatic pure-scatter furnace still returns EXACTLY 1 for ANY spectral σ_t (energy conservation, the unbiasedness oracle for the wavelength-resolved walk); a pure-absorbing slab reconstructs the SPECTRAL BEER INTEGRAL (1/Δλ)∫w(λ)·e^(−σ_t(λ)·2r)dλ matched to a fine deterministic quadrature and exits R>G>B (chromatic penetration, rendered); a spectral interior with achromatic parameters collapses exactly onto the scalar 12.0 walk (the reduction oracle); and a measured skin slab glows red end-to-end. Also (14.0) — IMPORTANCE SAMPLING OF MANY LIGHTS (THE LIGHT BVH): next-event estimation has to choose WHICH emitter to connect a shadow ray to, and Lumen used to pick one UNIFORMLY — perfect for one ceiling light, catastrophic for hundreds, where almost every shadow ray is spent on a far, occluded or back-facing emitter and the floor renders as a storm of colour noise. 14.0 fixes the distribution (not the sample count) with the production-standard answer — a LIGHT BVH (Conty Estevez & Kulla 2018, the technique now in Arnold and PBRT-v4): a binary tree over the emissive triangles whose every node caches its TOTAL POWER, its BOUNDING BOX, and a BOUNDING CONE OF EMITTER NORMALS. To pick a light for a shade point p, the sampler walks the tree from the root, at each step choosing the child in proportion to a cheap, conservative estimate of how much that CLUSTER could light p — importance = power·orientation(p)/distance²(p,box) — accumulating the product of branch probabilities as the EXACT selection pdf, which the MIS weight reuses; so near, bright, well-oriented lights win the lottery and the shadow rays land where the light actually is. It is a drop-in for the SELECTION STEP ONLY: sampleLight/lightPdf keep their contract, so the NEE↔BSDF MIS is untouched and the estimator stays UNBIASED — the tree only reshapes the variance of next-event estimation, never its mean, so a tree-sampled render converges to the SAME image a uniform one does, it just gets there far faster. Three guarantees make that airtight and PROVEN in the verify suite: the per-light selection probabilities SUM TO 1 at every p (a probability tree, normalised at each split); EVERY light keeps a strictly positive probability (a floored orientation term + clamped distance ⇒ no contributing light is ever excluded, the precondition for unbiasedness); and the SAMPLER MATCHES THE PDF to Monte-Carlo precision (the same branch probabilities drive sample and prob). It reduces EXACTLY to the uniform sampler when the clusters carry equal importance (a coincident-lights oracle nails 1/N to machine ε), and on a many-lights scene it cuts the NEE variance by HUNDREDS OF TIMES at equal mean. The uniform path is kept byte-for-byte as the default so every prior proof stays green; the tree is an opt-in toggle, on by default for two new showcase scenes — Star Field (a canopy of ~240 jewel-coloured emitters over a plaza) and Lantern Hall (a colonnade of warm lanterns where only the nearest few matter at any wall point). BDPT and the photon mapper sample lights their own way and are unaffected; the path tracer, the path-guided tracer and Metropolis all inherit the win. Five new proofs (75 total). Also (13.0) — PRACTICAL PATH GUIDING (THE SD-TREE): a FIFTH light-transport integrator that LEARNS its sampling distribution online (Müller, Gross & Novák 2017). As paths are traced, the radiance each carries is recorded into an SD-tree — a binary SPATIAL k-d tree over the scene whose every leaf owns a DIRECTIONAL quadtree over the sphere of directions, built on an EQUAL-AREA sphere↔square map so its area measure is proportional to solid angle with a constant Jacobian (a flux-proportional quadtree is therefore a radiance-proportional sampler with no cosine warp to undo). Both trees refine between iterations (each renders twice the samples of the last): the spatial tree subdivides where many paths PASS (a visit counter, so it grows even before any light is found — from 2 leaves to hundreds) and the directional tree sharpens where a bright direction APPEARS. At each surface the next direction is drawn from the MIXTURE α·p_bsdf+(1−α)·p_guide and weighted by that exact density, with the next-event-estimation and emission MIS weights using the same mixture pdf so every estimator stays consistent. Because the guide is a genuine probability distribution over the sphere (its density INTEGRATES TO 1, proven in the verify suite) the estimator is UNBIASED for any learned distribution — guiding only reshapes the variance, never the mean, so Guided converges to the SAME image as the other four (proven by a Guided≡path-tracer oracle on the diffuse box). Its home turf is light next-event estimation cannot sample: the new Glowing Orb scene is lit only by an emissive SPHERE (Lumen's NEE samples only triangle lights, so it is invisible to it) — blind BSDF sampling finds it ~0.5% of the time and the plain path tracer is all fireflies, while the guide learns the orb's direction per region (~9% hit rate) and renders it with measurably lower error at equal samples. Three engineering choices make it a real win rather than a naive net-negative: per-leaf MATURITY GATING (an under-trained quadtree samples nearly uniformly, which is worse than cosine BSDF sampling, so a region only guides once mature, else falls back to BSDF while still recording), VISIT-DRIVEN spatial subdivision, and a gentle α=0.7 (the piecewise-constant guide is a noisy sampler, so a 30% nudge atop 70% BSDF captures the win while bounding the variance, so guiding is never meaningfully worse than plain PT). Four new correctness proofs (DTree density ∫=1 over the sphere, sampler↔pdf to machine ε, ~100× variance cut on a peaked directional integral, and Guided≡PT on the box). Also (12.0) — SUBSURFACE SCATTERING: a dielectric can now carry an interior SCATTERING medium (scalar extinction σ_t, per-channel single-scattering albedo, Henyey–Greenstein g), turning glass into a TRANSLUCENT SOLID — marble, jade, wax, skin — that light refracts INTO, RANDOM-WALKS through and glows back OUT of. While the path is inside such a solid the integrator runs a homogeneous interior free-flight (distance ∼ e^(−σ_t·t)) bounded by the REAL surface geometry: a collision scatters off the phase function and multiplies throughput by the single-scattering albedo (so 1−albedo is absorbed at each step and the per-channel albedo IS the translucency's hue — why marble is warm-white and jade is green); reaching the boundary refracts the path out through the dielectric's exact Fresnel interface or TOTAL-INTERNALLY-REFLECTS it back in, trapping light to bounce again. It reuses the existing volume distance sampler, HG phase function and dielectric BSDF verbatim, so the transport loop is unchanged and all 61 prior proofs stay bit-for-bit green. The unmistakable subsurface glow a surface BRDF can never fake — the rim stays lit, the shadow side warms from within. Two showcase scenes (Subsurface Studio — a back-lit row of marble/jade/honey-wax/rose-quartz spheres; Jade Idol — a watertight hand-turned translucent lathe figurine) and FIVE correctness proofs: a pure-scattering index-matched furnace returns EXACTLY 1 for any phase g (zero variance — every path yields 1, proving the interior walk is unbiased and lossless), a pure-absorbing interior reproduces Beer's law e^(−σ·2r) straight from the free-flight sampler, the WHOLE translucent object (Fresnel boundary + TIR + multiple internal scattering) conserves energy to ≈1, a per-channel albedo tints the exitance R>G>B (the marble/jade/skin mechanism), and the reflectance rises strictly monotonically with the interior albedo — all bounded ≤1. Also (11.0) — REAL METALS FROM MEASURED COMPLEX IOR: a conductor's colour is not an RGB tint but a spectral reflectance set by its complex refractive index n̄(λ)=η(λ)−ik(λ), so Lumen now carries measured η/k spectra (gold/silver/copper from Johnson & Christy, aluminium from Rakić, iron & chromium) and evaluates the EXACT unpolarised conductor Fresnel at the path's committed hero wavelength — the same machinery that disperses glass — so a metal's hue emerges spectrally: gold rims warm, copper reads red, silver stays bright-neutral and aluminium leans faintly blue, with the correct grazing desaturation that Schlick-from-RGB can never capture. The complex-IOR Fresnel rides on every existing lobe (single-scatter GGX, anisotropic brushed metal, AND the Kulla–Conty multiscatter compensation) through one shared FresnelSpec, gated behind an optional spectrum field so the prior 55 proofs stay bit-for-bit green. New Metals of the World scene + six proofs — R∈[0,1] & grazing→1, the k→0 limit reducing EXACTLY to the dielectric Fresnel, the textbook metal colours, a furnace render reconstructing the measured RGB head-on, sampler↔pdf↔weight MIS consistency, and spectral multiscatter energy bounded by the hemispherical-average reflectance F̄. Also (10.0) — a physically based MATERIAL SYSTEM that makes the surfaces as rigorous as the light transport: ENERGY-CONSERVING ROUGH METAL via a Kulla–Conty multiple-scattering compensation lobe (driven by the GGX directional albedo E(μ,α) Monte-Carlo–integrated into a table at start-up) so rough gold stops darkening to grey and stays bright and saturated — proven in-app to reflect ≈1 in a white furnace with compensation and measurably less without; ANISOTROPIC (brushed/milled) METAL with two GGX roughness axes in a rotatable tangent frame so the highlight stretches into a streak, reciprocity and energy preserved exactly; OREN–NAYAR rough-diffuse for chalk/clay/plaster/the-moon flat-matte look; and CLEAR-COAT layered materials (glazed ceramic, lacquer, car paint) — a GGX dielectric coat over a Lambert/Oren–Nayar base, the two lobes sampled by Fresnel weight with a combined pdf so the stack conserves energy AND stays MIS-consistent (the new BSDFs match BDPT pixel-for-pixel in the verify suite). Three new showcase scenes — Brushed Metal, Rough Conductors (a single-scatter vs multiscatter split), and Ceramics & Clay — plus six new correctness proofs. It carries FIVE selectable light-transport integrators: a unidirectional Monte-Carlo path tracer, the path-guided path tracer above (the learned SD-tree), a full bidirectional path tracer (BDPT) — Veach–Guibas camera×light vertex connections weighted by balance-heuristic multiple importance sampling, which renders indirect-lit scenes (an emitter hidden in a cove) at a fraction of the noise — primary-sample-space Metropolis light transport (PSSMLT), a Markov chain run over the path tracer's own random-number stream (Kelemen mutation + Metropolis-Hastings acceptance, bootstrap normalisation, expected-value splatting, parallel chains across the worker pool) that locks onto the hardest-to-find light and refines the whole frame at once — the path tracer is reused verbatim as the contribution function because the Metropolis sampler simply IS the RNG — AND stochastic progressive photon mapping (SPPM), which runs the transport BACKWARDS to resolve the CAUSTICS the other three sample terribly (light focused through glass onto a floor — a light→specular→diffuse path next-event estimation can't connect through the lens): photons are emitted from the area lights (power-sampled to equal flux), refracted through the specular geometry and deposited at per-pixel measurement points via an exact CSR spatial hash, while a jittered camera pass each iteration places those points and the per-point gather radius is shrunk on the Hachisuka schedule R²←R²·(N+αM)/(N+M) so the density-estimation bias provably vanishes and the estimate converges. Because all four estimators solve the same rendering equation, the in-app verification suite proves they converge to the SAME image (checking PSSMLT and SPPM against the path tracer on both global brightness and spatial distribution), that SPPM resolves a finite focused caustic the others miss, that the photon-gather grid returns exactly the in-radius points vs brute force, and that the MIS weights form an exact partition of unity. The photon mapper is also SPECTRAL — a photon commits a random hero wavelength on its first dispersive hit so the caustic it focuses through glass fans into a RAINBOW (proven to conserve total energy while spreading into colour) — and DAYLIGHT-complete: the sun is itself a photon emitter (photons leave a disc sized to the scene's bounding sphere as a parallel beam), so daylight scenes get photon-mapped SUN CAUSTICS, with the distant-light flux normalisation proven against the path tracer. Showcase scenes include a Caustic Pool — a sine-ripple water surface (analytic smooth normals) throwing the shifting bright filaments of a swimming pool. Plus participating media (fog/smoke/cloud) with Henyey-Greenstein scattering and volumetric god rays, thin-film interference for soap-bubble/oil-slick iridescence, smooth triangle meshes (icosphere, lathe & OBJ import) with barycentric-interpolated normals, a SAH bounding volume hierarchy, GGX microfacet BSDFs with VNDF sampling, smooth and frosted dielectrics, spectral dispersion (prism rainbows), Beer-Lambert volumetric absorption, procedural textures, an analytic Preetham daylight sky whose sun is a sampled light, next-event estimation with multiple importance sampling, scrambled-Halton low-discrepancy sampling, adaptive variance-guided sampling with a live noise heatmap, a multi-threaded Web Worker pool, ACES tone mapping, and an edge-avoiding À-Trous denoiser — global illumination on the CPU, in your browser. The participating media are now HETEROGENEOUS too: procedural 3D fBm density fields (cumulus clouds, rising smoke plumes, exponential ground-fog layers) traced unbiasedly with the null-collision estimators — DELTA TRACKING (Woodcock free-flight sampling against a constant majorant, accepting a real scatter with probability σ(x)/σ̄) for in-volume collisions and RATIO TRACKING for shadow-ray transmittance — so a cloud self-shadows into soft greys, flares a forward-scattering silver lining toward the sun, and breaks light into real volumetric beams, all from one continuously varying field, with five new proofs (delta tracking reproduces e^(−σL), ratio tracking matches a fine quadrature of a varying optical depth, and a heterogeneous pure-scattering volume conserves energy exactly). Media can also EMIT light volumetrically — at a real collision the path collects (1−albedo)·Lₑ of self-radiance, so a heterogeneous field glows brightest in its dense core (a soft, physically integrated fireball, not a billboard), proven to obey the emission–absorption law (1−e^(−σ_t·chord))·Lₑ; see the glowing Ember scene.
